text.title: Spatial Computing Overview Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the 2026 platform-selection decision record. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every platform score MUST cite a 2025-or-newer source for install base, SDK status, and store policy. Pre-2024 data MUST NOT be used as authoritative.

rationale.source: IDC 2025 AR/VR tracker; vendor 2025 SEC filings

rule.rationale: Platform landscape changed sharply in 2024-2026 (Vision Pro launch, Android XR announcement, Pico contraction); stale data produces wrong decisions.

rule.statement: Each platform candidate MUST be scored 0-5 on install_base, sdk_maturity, audience_fit, content_type_fit, store_constraints. Each score MUST have a one-line evidence string.
rule.rationale: Single-score "best platform" recommendations cannot be defended later when ROI is challenged.

rule.statement: Recommendation MUST name one primary platform AND at least one fallback (cross-platform target via WebXR or porting plan). Single-platform lock-in MUST include an explicit risk note.
rule.rationale: Single-platform launches are vulnerable to vendor pivots; a fallback path is required for risk parity.

rule.statement: The decision MUST map use-case content type (productivity floating windows / cinematic video / dialogue agent / interactive simulation) to the platform's strength. Mismatches MUST be called out as risks.

rationale.source: Apple visionOS HIG; Meta Horizon docs

rule.rationale: Vision Pro excels at productivity + cinematic; Quest excels at active gaming; HoloLens at enterprise overlay. Forcing the wrong content type onto a platform yields poor UX.

rule.statement: Audience profile (consumer vs prosumer vs enterprise) MUST match platform price point and distribution. Decision record MUST cite expected ARPU + addressable installed base.
rule.rationale: Consumer apps on $3500 Vision Pro miss audience; enterprise XR apps on Quest 3 require MDM provisioning that consumer headsets do not support.
